chunk_summary: Children with congenital heart disease (CHD) face feeding fatigue and growth challenges due to elevated energy needs and limited intake. Strategies include high-calorie diets, paced feeding, and early escalation to nutritional support when oral intake is insufficient.
learning_objectives: Understand the impact of congenital heart disease on feeding and growth in children.
Identify signs of feeding fatigue and strategies to optimize energy intake in CHD patients.
Learn dietary modifications, including high-calorie strategies and feeding techniques, for children with CHD.
Recognize when to escalate to oral nutritional supplements (ONS) or nasogastric (NG) feeding.
key_facts: Children with CHD have elevated energy needs but limited intake due to tachypnea, fatigue, and fluid restrictions.
Feeding strategies include smaller, frequent meals and calorie-dense additions like ghee, oil, or groundnut paste.
Feeding observation should assess uncoordinated suck-swallow-breathe, sweating, or frequent pauses.
Supervised formula concentration adjustments should only occur with cardiology and dietitian oversight.
Escalate to oral nutritional supplements (ONS) or NG feeding if growth targets are not met or feeding fatigue is severe.
Evidence-based supportive care measures are essential
reference_quote: Children with CHD are at high risk of faltering growth because energy needs are elevated while intake is limited by tachypnea, early fatigue, and fluid restriction. (Pediatric Medicine)
document_key_topics: Feeding fatigue in congenital heart disease
